C:
For a content-heavy marketing site, full server-side rendering is usually **not** worth the operational complexity — but pre-rendering almost certainly is. The distinction matters more than the SSR-vs-SPA framing suggests.

Here's the reasoning.

**What you actually need is pre-rendered HTML, not a render server.** Marketing content (landing pages, blog posts, product pages) changes on a publish cadence, not per request. That means static site generation (SSG) gets you everything SSR is usually invoked for — crawlable HTML in the initial response, fast first contentful paint, correct Open Graph/Twitter card previews for social and Slack unfurls — without running and scaling a Node render tier. You build HTML at deploy time and serve it from a CDN. That's strictly less complexity than a client-only SPA, not more.

**SSR's real cost is the runtime server.** Per-request rendering means a live Node process on the hot path: cold starts, memory under traffic spikes, a new class of 500s, cache-invalidation logic, and hydration mismatches to debug. For mostly-static content you'd be paying that bill to re-render identical HTML on every hit — which is why teams end up bolting caching back on top, reinventing SSG the hard way.

**SSR earns its keep only when content is genuinely per-request:** personalized pages (logged-in dashboards, geo/AB content that must be correct in the first byte for SEO), data too fresh or too large to rebuild on publish, or thousands of pages where full rebuilds get slow. A marketing site rarely hits these.

**The pragmatic middle ground** is a hybrid framework (Next.js, Astro, SvelteKit, Nuxt) that defaults to static and lets you opt specific routes into SSR or ISR (incremental static regeneration — periodic background re-rendering). You get static simplicity for the 95% and a per-request escape hatch for the few pages that need it, without committing the whole site to a render server. Astro is worth a particular look for content-heavy marketing because it ships near-zero JS by default.

Bottom line: skip standalone SSR. Reach for SSG/static-first, with selective ISR or SSR only on the routes that provably need fresh-per-request data. The SEO and performance wins you're after come from pre-rendering, and you can get those at the cheaper end of the complexity curve.

One thing that would sharpen this: is any part of the site personalized or gated (pricing by region, logged-in state, frequently-changing inventory)? If not, static-first is a clear call. If yes, that's exactly where the selective SSR escape hatch pays off.

For a content-heavy marketing site, full server-side rendering is usually **not** worth the operational complexity — but pre-rendering almost certainly is. The distinction matters more than the SSR-vs-SPA framing suggests.

Here's the reasoning.

**What you actually need is pre-rendered HTML, not a render server.** Marketing content (landing pages, blog posts, product pages) changes on a publish cadence, not per request. That means static site generation (SSG) gets you everything SSR is usually invoked for — crawlable HTML in the initial response, fast first contentful paint, correct Open Graph/Twitter card previews for social and Slack unfurls — without running and scaling a Node render tier. You build HTML at deploy time and serve it from a CDN. That's strictly less complexity than a client-only SPA, not more.

**SSR's real cost is the runtime server.** Per-request rendering means a live Node process on the hot path: cold starts, memory under traffic spikes, a new class of 500s, cache-invalidation logic, and hydration mismatches to debug. For mostly-static content you'd be paying that bill to re-render identical HTML on every hit — which is why teams end up bolting caching back on top, reinventing SSG the hard way.

**SSR earns its keep only when content is genuinely per-request:** personalized pages (logged-in dashboards, geo/AB content that must be correct in the first byte for SEO), data too fresh or too large to rebuild on publish, or thousands of pages where full rebuilds get slow. A marketing site rarely hits these.

**The pragmatic middle ground** is a hybrid framework (Next.js, Astro, SvelteKit, Nuxt) that defaults to static and lets you opt specific routes into SSR or ISR (incremental static regeneration — periodic background re-rendering). You get static simplicity for the 95% and a per-request escape hatch for the few pages that need it, without committing the whole site to a render server. Astro is worth a particular look for content-heavy marketing because it ships near-zero JS by default.

**What would flip this recommendation.** This call rests on one assumption: that your content is essentially the same for every visitor and updates on a publish cadence. The conclusion changes if that breaks down — if a meaningful share of pages must be personalized or gated in the first byte (region-based pricing, logged-in state, A/B variants that need to be correct for SEO), if inventory or data changes faster than you can rebuild, or if your page count grows large enough that full static rebuilds become painfully slow. The honest limit of this advice is that it's a default for the common case, not a verdict on your specific content model; if you're near any of those edges, the static-first call weakens and the selective-SSR escape hatch starts carrying real weight. The cheapest way to find out is to count how many routes actually need fresh-per-request data — if it's a handful, static-first with selective ISR still wins; if it's most of the site, you were never really building a static marketing site.

Bottom line: skip standalone SSR. Reach for SSG/static-first, with selective ISR or SSR only on the routes that provably need fresh-per-request data. The SEO and performance wins you're after come from pre-rendering, and you can get those at the cheaper end of the complexity curve.

One thing that would sharpen this: is any part of the site personalized or gated (pricing by region, logged-in state, frequently-changing inventory)? If not, static-first is a clear call. If yes, that's exactly where the selective SSR escape hatch pays off.
